narrative_blocks.header: Farmer in Bolton
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon Benjamin Talcott was a farmer in Bolton, Connecticut, settled on land given to him by his father. He and his wife Elizabeth Lyman had eleven children. Elizabeth died September 3, 1810; Deacon Benjamin died April 18, 1811 — barely seven months after her.

burial.plot_notes: North Bolton Cemetery, Bamforth Road, Bolton CT. Consistent with his land near the quarry on Bolton Road. SOURCE: Tankerhoosen Valley History.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmer in Bolton
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon Benjamin Talcott was a farmer in Bolton, Connecticut, settled on land given to him by his father. He and his wife Elizabeth Lyman had eleven children. Elizabeth died September 3, 1810; Deacon Benjamin died April 18, 1811 — barely seven months after her.

burial.plot_notes: Old Burial Ground of North Bolton (Bamforth Road), Bolton CT. Gravestone inscription: "Sacred to the memory of Mrs. Elisabeth; consort of Dea. Benjamin Talcott; who departed this life Sept 3rd 1810, in the 81st year of her age. She opened her mouth with wisdom; and in her tongue was the law of kindness. Her children [arise] up, and call her blessed."

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Lyman and Elizabeth Smith of Coventry CT. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

narrative_blocks.header: "She opened her mouth with wisdom"
narrative_blocks.body: The gravestone of Elisabeth Lyman Talcott in the Old Burial Ground of North Bolton reads: "Sacred to the memory of Mrs. Elisabeth; consort of Dea. Benjamin Talcott; who departed this life Sept 3rd 1810, in the 81st year of her age. She opened her mouth with wisdom; and in her tongue was the law of kindness. Her children arise up, and call her blessed."

narrative_blocks.header: He and brother Elijah married sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Talcott married Jerusha Olmstead on October 31, 1782; his brother Elijah had married her sister Rebecca Olmstead three years earlier. Jerusha died in 1800; Benjamin married twice more, outliving all three wives.

quotes.text: Farmer in comfortable circumstances, Vernon CT.

parents.father_research_notes: Nathaniel and Sarah Pitkin Olmstead. Sister Rebecca Olmstead married T01029 Deacon Elijah Talcott (Benjamin's brother). SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.
Percival family of East Haddam CT.
Daughter of Daniel Field. Previously widow of Samuel Baker. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Quarryville Cemetery, Bolton CT. GPS: 41.7961009, -72.4309397.

narrative_blocks.header: Married an Olmstead — so did his brother Benjamin
narrative_blocks.body: Elijah Talcott married Rebecca Olmstead on November 17, 1779. Three years later his brother Benjamin married Rebecca's sister Jerusha Olmstead. Two Talcott brothers married two Olmstead sisters — a pattern the family repeated in the generation above, when two Wright cousins married two of their father's children.

burial.plot_notes: Quarryville Cemetery, Bolton CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID 10706544.

parents.father_research_notes: Nathaniel and Sarah Pitkin Olmstead. Her sister Jerusha Olmstead married T01028 Benjamin Talcott (T01029's brother). SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

narrative_blocks.header: Her sister married her husband's brother
narrative_blocks.body: Rebecca Olmstead married Deacon Elijah Talcott in 1779. Three years later, her sister Jerusha Olmstead married Elijah's brother Benjamin Talcott. Two Olmstead sisters from the same family married two Talcott brothers — a pattern that echoed the Wright-Wright-Talcott-Talcott double marriage of the previous generation.

burial.plot_notes: Quarryville Cemetery, Bolton CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID 10706544.

narrative_blocks.header: She brought a fortune to her marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Ruth Wright was an only child who inherited a large fortune from her wealthy father and brought it to her marriage with Elizur Talcott. Family memory described her as a remarkably handsome woman, tall and erect, with golden hair and black eyes.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Talcottville CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Deacon in Talcottville
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon Flavel Talcott lived and died in Talcottville, buried at Mount Hope Cemetery. Twelve years after his death in 1844, his cousins Charles Denison and Horace Wells Talcott would purchase the Talcottville mills and transform the village into a manufacturing center.

burial.plot_notes: Died in Warren CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Dartmouth 1812 — settled at Clinton, then Warren CT
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Hart Talcott graduated Dartmouth College in 1812 and was ordained to the ministry. He was settled first at Kittingsworth (now Clinton), Connecticut, and then at Warren, Connecticut. His first wife Parthenia Brainard died in Clinton in 1823.

death.cause: consumption contracted by exposure during medical practice

burial.plot_notes: Winsted Old Burying Ground, Winsted, Litchfield County CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale graduate and physician — died at 37
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. William Olmstead Talcott graduated Yale College in 1823, became a physician, and died on October 27, 1831, in Winchester CT at age 37. His daughter Elizabeth O. was born September 1 — less than two months before his death. His middle name "Olmstead" honored his mother Rebecca Olmstead.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died 12 years before him
narrative_blocks.body: Julius Talcott married Maria Schoonmaker in 1824; she died January 22, 1842, leaving him a widower for the last 12 years of his life. Of their four children — Edward Charles, Parthenia Maria, Elijah Julius, and Eliza Jane — only Edward Charles (b.1824) had a clear record of living past 30. Julius died October 10, 1854.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery, Vernon CT. SOURCE: Tankerhoosen Valley History.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 56, leaving children still in diapers
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Talcott died May 26, 1813 at age 56 — two years after his father Deacon Benjamin. At his death his oldest son was 20 and his youngest son James had just been born. He left an estate valued at $5,215, including a home lot and buildings worth $1,650.

narrative_blocks.header: Named replacement children after two he lost
narrative_blocks.body: Their first daughter Sarah died at ten days old in March 1796, and the next daughter, born fourteen months later, was given the same name. The pattern repeated with James Tudor — when the first one died at three months in 1811, they gave the name to their final son, born two years later in 1813.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery, Vernon CT.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Tudor Smith of Cromwell CT

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery, Vernon CT. He and Harriet Talcott buried together.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded the Granite Mills paper company on the

institutions.institution_blurb: Founded and owned the Granite Mills paper company on the Hockanum River.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-owned the Granite Mills in Talcottville at the time of the 1881 fire.

narrative_blocks.header: Built the Granite Mills
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Smith Talcott founded the Granite Mills on the lower Hockanum River in Talcottville, establishing a linseed oil operation around 1838 that was converted to paper manufacturing after an 1863 fire. The mill purchased old paper in bales from New York, Boston, and Philadelphia, recycling it into binder boards, album covers, and press paper — running day and night, producing up to 25 tons a week. His middle name "Smith" honored his mother Sarah Smith; his mills neighbored, but were entirely separate from, the Talcottville mill of his distant cousins.

narrative_blocks.header: The mill burned four times
narrative_blocks.body: The Granite Mills were destroyed by fire in 1863, 1870, and 1881, rebuilt each time. After the 1870 fire the Tolland County Journal noted "there is enterprise enough remaining there to rebuild." The final fire came August 1, 1912 — started by spontaneous combustion in 75 tons of waste paper. The mill was not rebuilt.

burial.plot_notes: East Cemetery, Manchester CT.
Rushford Cemetery, Allegany Co NY.

narrative_blocks.header: Yankee peddler turned Cemetery President
narrative_blocks.body: Before settling in western New York, Elihu worked the South as a Yankee peddler, selling goods from a wagon. He became the first President of the Rushford Cemetery Association in 1850, sixteen years before being buried there himself.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Auburn Cemetery, Cambridge MA. Interred Dec 31, 1873; lot owner. No gravestone or inscription.
Southwest Cemetery, Vernon CT.
Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped write the Connecticut Constitution of 1818
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon Phineas Talcott of Vernon served in the Connecticut General Assembly for many sessions and was one of the delegates who formed the Connecticut State Constitution of 1818 — the document that replaced the colonial charter of 1662 and separated church from state. His wife Hannah died just nineteen days after him, on January 5, 1836.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter founded a Japanese college
narrative_blocks.body: Through his son Ralph, co-founder of Rockville, Connecticut, came granddaughter Eliza Talcott — founder of Kobe College in Japan. The deacon’s line ran from Bolton’s meetinghouse to Meiji-era missionary education in two generations.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. FindAGrave confirms. Died 19 days after her husband.

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Rev. Ebenezer Kellogg and Hannah Wright. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

death.cause: head injury — fall on street, concussion of brain

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. Buried at side of wife Lora, whom he had mourned for 31 years.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded American Mills; probate judge; state senator

institutions.institution_blurb: Steward 1828-1835; helped pioneer moral care with Eli Todd.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He donated the land; became Talcott Park after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Steward of America's pioneering psychiatric hospital
narrative_blocks.body: From 1828 to 1835, Phineas Talcott Jr. served as steward of the Hartford Retreat for the Insane, one of only four such facilities in the country. He worked alongside Dr. Eli Todd, the first superintendent, helping develop a program of moral care that achieved the highest cure rate in the nation. His wife Lora was the beloved matron; she died at the Retreat in 1835.

narrative_blocks.header: Senator, judge, and mill president at once
narrative_blocks.body: Phineas served as Rockville's judge of probate for sixteen years across four terms, Connecticut State Senator in 1851, and president of the Rockville Railroad Company — while simultaneously running the American Mills, the Savings Bank of Rockville, the Aqueduct Company, and two mill interests. His home's left wing was his courtroom.

narrative_blocks.header: Rode west on horseback after losing Lora
narrative_blocks.body: After Lora's death in January 1835, he and his brother Ralph rode west on horseback in November — Buffalo to Sandusky, then on to Quincy, Illinois, prospecting for land. He bought no property and returned to Rockville, where within fifteen months he had become agent of the Rock Manufacturing Company under his father-in-law Francis McLean.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of a fall, buried beside Lora
narrative_blocks.body: Phineas Talcott Jr. died June 6, 1863 when a fall on the street caused a concussion of the brain. He was described as 'a man of heavy weight and commanding figure.' He was buried at the side of his wife Lora in Elmwood Cemetery — she had died 31 years earlier, and he had never remarried.

sources.label: Commemorative Biographical Record of Tolland and Windham Counties CT, 1903, p.102
Tankerhoosen Valley History — Phineas Talcott Jr.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: His mill turned barren fields into a city
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph Talcott co-founded Rockville, Connecticut, where he and a partner started manufacturing cloth by machinery — the first such operation in the town. The Talcott Pedigree records: "where in 1830 there were only barren fields, there is now in 1875 a populous, picturesque and beautiful city." His daughter Eliza would go on to found a college in Japan.

burial.plot_notes: Log Plain Cemetery, Greenfield, Franklin County MA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: One child scalded to death at five
narrative_blocks.body: Their son Talcott Griswold died in 1796 at age five after being scalded — a fatal household accident recorded in the Talcott Pedigree. Elizabeth outlived her husband Theophilus by twenty-two years, dying in 1843.

burial.plot_notes: Log Plain Cemetery, Greenfield MA.
Log Plain Cemetery Greenfield MA (likely).

narrative_blocks.header: Married Robert Clark in Greenfield at 17
narrative_blocks.body: Phila Griswold was born August 6, 1785 in Greenfield, Massachusetts, the eldest child of Elizabeth Talcott Griswold and Revolutionary War veteran Theophilus Griswold. She married Robert Clark on June 26, 1803, at age 17, in Greenfield. They had two children: Philena Ann, who lived to 86 and died in Minnesota, and John Wrisley, who died in Colrain MA.

burial.plot_notes: Log Plain Cemetery Greenfield MA (likely).
Log Plain Cemetery Greenfield MA (likely).

narrative_blocks.header: Died at five — scalded to death
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott Griswold, named for his mother's Talcott family, died in 1796 at age five having been scalded — a fatal household accident recorded in the Talcott Pedigree. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Log Plain Cemetery, Greenfield MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin of the minister
narrative_blocks.body: Lyman Griswold was born April 5, 1793 in Greenfield — twin of his brother Rev. John Flavel Griswold. While his twin brother travelled through Massachusetts, Vermont, and New Hampshire as a Congregationalist minister, Lyman remained in Greenfield, dying there in 1863 aged 69. His wife Urania Barrett outlived him by 17 years.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Bernardston, Franklin County MA. Summer home in later life.

narrative_blocks.header: Andover 1824 — five pastorates across four New
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. John Flavel Griswold graduated Andover Theological Seminary in 1824 and served congregations in Massachusetts, Vermont, and New Hampshire over thirty years. He died in Brooklyn in 1872, buried at Center Cemetery in Bernardston, Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin of Lyman
narrative_blocks.body: John Flavel and his twin Lyman were both born in Greenfield in 1793. Lyman stayed in Greenfield until 1863; John Flavel died in Brooklyn in 1872, buried at Center Cemetery in Bernardston, Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a Puritan preacher
narrative_blocks.body: John Flavel Griswold was named for the English Puritan minister John Flavel (c.1627-1691), a Presbyterian author and preacher whose devotional works were widely read in New England.

sources.label: Yale Alumni Obituary Manuscript — Rev. John Flavel Griswold

institutions.institution_blurb: Andover Theological Seminary, class of 1824. Ordained 1825.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow followed their daughter to Illinois
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Talcott died in 1807 at 45, leaving his second wife Abigail Holt with two young children. Decades later Abigail followed their daughter Abigail Talcott Hubbard to the Wethersfield settlement in Illinois — named by Connecticut emigrants for their home county — where all three are buried together.

parents.father_research_notes: Father identified in Talcott Pedigree as "Tel Keeney" — likely Theophilus Keeney.

burial.plot_notes: Wethersfield Cemetery, Kewanee (formerly Wethersfield), Henry County IL. She followed her daughter Abigail (T02215) west to Illinois.

parents.father_research_notes: Deacon Nathaniel Holt. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed in Connecticut — followed her daughter to Illinois
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Holt was widowed when Daniel Talcott died in 1807, leaving her in Connecticut. She later followed daughter Abigail Talcott Hubbard to the Illinois Wethersfield settlement and died there in 1844, buried alongside her daughter and son-in-law.

burial.plot_notes: Wethersfield Cemetery, Kewanee (formerly Wethersfield), Henry County IL.

narrative_blocks.header: The Illinois town was named Wethersfield
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Talcott moved to the Wethersfield settlement in Illinois, named by Connecticut emigrants for the ancestral Talcott town. Her mother Abigail Holt followed west and all three — Abigail, her husband Jeduthan Hubbard, and her mother — are buried there together.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery, Vernon CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Royal died at 26
narrative_blocks.body: Jacob's son Royal married Martha Warner and their daughter Harriet Martha Ann was born in July 1823 — Royal died October 9, 1823, aged 26. Harriet died in 1826 at two; Martha Warner died in 1827: three generations gone in four years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on November 27
narrative_blocks.body: Jacob Talcott died November 27, 1835. His wife Anna Carpenter died November 27, 1849 — the same date, fourteen years apart.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery, Vernon CT. Died exactly 14 years to the day after her husband Jacob.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on the same date as her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jacob Talcott died November 27, 1835. His wife Anna Carpenter died November 27, 1849 — the same day of the same month, fourteen years to the day later.

narrative_blocks.body: Harriet Martha Ann was born in July 1823; Royal Talcott died October 9, 1823, aged 26. Harriet died in March 1826; her mother Martha Warner died in August 1827.

burial.plot_notes: Quarryville Cemetery, Bolton CT. GPS: 41.7961009, -72.4309397.

narrative_blocks.body: Rachel Talcott Howard died September 24, 1805, aged 39. Her son Anson was born that same day.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Howard was born in Bolton, Connecticut in 1800 and moved west to Perry, New York. Her son Sidney Ward served as captain in the 136th New York Infantry and later practiced law in New York City until 1904.

burial.plot_notes: Grace Cemetery, Castile, Wyoming County NY.

narrative_blocks.header: Born the day his mother died
narrative_blocks.body: Anson Howard was born September 24, 1805, in Bolton, Connecticut. His mother Rachel Talcott Howard died that same day, aged 39.

sources.label: The Castilian newspaper, Castile NY, January 31, 1924

narrative_blocks.header: Two infant deaths, two daughters who died single
narrative_blocks.body: Olive Talcott had nine children with David Smith in Vernon; two died in infancy (1795, 1796), two daughters died young and single. Son Daniel moved to the Adirondacks of New York; Olive died May 19, 1829.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery, Vernon CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: First wife died after three years
narrative_blocks.body: David Talcott Smith's first wife Mary McLean died in 1831, three years after their marriage. Their son Francis, born in 1829, outlived his mother by 90 years, dying in Hartford in 1919.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT (likely).
Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT (likely).
Chilson Community Cemetery, Ticonderoga area, Essex County NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: From Vernon CT to the Adirondacks
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Smith was born in Vernon, Connecticut in 1810 and moved to the Chilson hamlet in Essex County, New York, near Ticonderoga in the Adirondack foothills. He died there in 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Burial location unknown. Possibly Perry NY area.

narrative_blocks.header: Son was named Talcott — a third Keeney-Talcott marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Esther named her son Talcott Keeney for the Talcott family. Her husband Alexander Keeney was from Perry, New York — the same town where her sister Rachel's husband Samuel Howard had settled.

parents.father_research_notes: Keeney family of Bolton/Vernon CT area. Likely related to "Tel Keeney" (I00221 Betsey Keeney's father, who married T01033 Daniel Talcott). Alexander is described as "of Perry NY" — the same Perry, Wyoming County NY where Samuel Howard (I00227, husband of T01035 Rachel Talcott) also settled. Two of Deacon Benjamin's daughters married into families with Perry NY connections. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Talcott — honoring his mother's family
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott Keeney, born June 11, 1802, was named for his mother's Talcott family. He married three times and died in November 1859. His name preserved the Talcott connection into the Keeney line.

narrative_blocks.header: Samuel Lyman — carrying two ancestral names
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Lyman Keeney combined the names of two family lines: Samuel for the Talcott Samuel line ( Deacon Samuel), and Lyman for his maternal grandmother Elizabeth Lyman Talcott. He married Sarah Reddish in 1833 and died August 14, 1863.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT (likely).
Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmer and father of Yale's physician-genealogist
narrative_blocks.body: Alvan Talcott farmed Bolton and Vernon; his son Alvan Jr. labored on the farm in summer and studied in winter until entering Yale College class of 1824. Alvan Sr. died in 1835 before his son endowed a Yale professorship.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Root of Vernon CT. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT (likely).

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her aunt Rachel Howard
narrative_blocks.body: Rachel Howard Talcott (b. Oct 2, 1806) was named for her aunt Rachel Talcott Howard, who died September 24, 1805 — just over a year before this niece was born. Her middle name Howard preserved her aunt's married name in the next generation.

burial.plot_notes: West Side Cemetery Guilford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale BA 1824; Yale MD 1831. In 1888 created and endowed a professorship at Yale Medical School bearing his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmed all summer — graduated Yale, then Yale Medical
narrative_blocks.body: Alvan Talcott worked on his father's farm in summer and studied only in fall and winter, graduating Yale College class of 1824 and Yale Medical School in 1831.

narrative_blocks.header: He endowed the Alvan Talcott Professor of Classics
narrative_blocks.body: In 1888, Alvan Talcott endowed the Alvan Talcott Professor of Classics at Yale. In October 2016, Egbert Bakker — a scholar of Ancient Greek literature — was named to that chair.

narrative_blocks.header: Son lost at sea
narrative_blocks.body: William Henry's packet ship left England in 1856 and was never heard from again. Both daughters also predeceased him; Alvan Talcott died in Guilford on January 17, 1891.

sources.label: New Haven Colony Historical Society, Mss. 91 — Alvan Talcott Papers (1831-1889)
Yale Faculty Announcement — Egbert Bakker named Alvan Talcott Professor of Classics, Oct 24, 2016

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT (likely).

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 26
narrative_blocks.body: Rosanna Talcott's first son William Stanley was born and died in 1837. Her second son Edward Talcott was born April 3, 1838; Rosanna died July 16, 1838, aged 26.

burial.plot_notes: Southwest Cemetery Vernon CT (likely).

narrative_blocks.header: First Talcott in Bolton
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Talcott was the first of the Glastonbury Talcotts to move to Bolton, farming land his father Deacon Benjamin had willed him in 1727. As a Bolton Proprietor (1731 patent), he received one-and-a-half parts — his holdings near Bolton Road and the quarry became the base from which the entire Bolton/Vernon line descends. He had twelve children by two wives — Esther Lyman and Deborah Gillett — "from whom most of the Bolton/Vernon line of Talcotts descends."

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children seeded the Bolton Talcotts
narrative_blocks.body: Granted land in the 1731 Bolton patent, he farmed near the quarry and gave ground to his son beside it. Five children by his first wife and seven by his second became the root of nearly the whole Bolton and Vernon line of the family.

bio.bio_blurb: First Talcott to settle in Bolton

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor, Hartford County CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

will.summary: Will of Lt. Benjamin Talcott, Glastonbury CT. Wife: £80 + use of parlor, cellar, garret, ovens, gardens, one-third of orchard and land west of highway to great river — during widowhood only. Son Benjamin: lands in Bolton + 124 acres Hebron (bought of Joseph and John Phelps). Son John: 300 acres Hebron (west of Jacob Root), 50 acres Hebron (north of Richard Curtis), one yoke of steers, £10 moveable, + £15 from Elizur. Son Samuel: south half of home lot, 153 acres beyond Black Ledge River (Glastonbury/Hebron border), half undivided Glastonbury lands, smith-shop with all smithery tools, longest gun, half team. Son Elizur: north half of home lot with all buildings (except smith-shop), 60 acres Ash Swamp Glastonbury, 96 acres near bare hill Glastonbury, other half undivided lands, newest gun, half team — he to pay John £15. Daughter Sarah: £100. Daughter Hannah: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Daughter Mehitabel: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Executors: son-in-law Jonathan Hale and son Benjamin Talcott. Witnesses: Samuel Smith Jr., Nathaniel Talcott, Thomas Wells. Estate inventory: £2563 15s. 8d.
will.note: Will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.106-109.

cross_connections.display_label: Glastonbury homestead passed intact to him 175 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His house bore bullet marks until 1854
narrative_blocks.body: He built a fortified wooden house on Main Street, Glastonbury in 1699 — a refuge from Indian attacks for the surrounding community. The house bore bullet marks until it was taken down soon after 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died in childbed at thirty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister, granddaughter of original 1636 Hartford settler John Hollister, married him in 1699 and bore him eight children. She died in childbirth at the homestead October 15, 1715.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons signed Bolton's 1731 founding patent
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons Benjamin and Nathaniel were listed as Bolton Proprietors in the 1731 patent, signed by their cousin Governor Joseph Talcott. A third son Elizur later chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings
narrative_blocks.body: His son Elizur married Ruth Wright; his daughter Mehitabel married Ruth's first cousin Hezekiah Wright. Two Wright cousins thus married two Talcott siblings, weaving the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Farm in family from 1643 through 1876
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John Talcott had bought the Glastonbury farm from Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve in 1643. The land was still owned by descendant Jared G. Talcott in 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in childbed; the baby followed
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister married Deacon Benjamin Talcott and died in childbed at Glastonbury in 1715, at thirty-eight. The daughter whose birth she did not survive, Abigail, lived only eighteen days more.

burialCemetery.name: Old Burial Ground of North Bolton (Bamforth Road)
burialCemetery.notes: Bamforth Road, Bolton CT (also known as North Bolton Cemetery). Burial site of T00909 Deacon Benjamin Talcott (1725-1811) and I00207 Elizabeth Lyman Talcott (1730-1810). Gravestone inscription for Elizabeth: "Sacred to the memory of Mrs. Elisabeth; consort of Dea. Benjamin Talcott; who departed this life Sept 3rd 1810, in the 81st year of her age. She opened her mouth with wisdom; and in her tongue was the law of kindness. Her children [arise] up, and call her blessed."
